body.section:
Functions
To make certain sections of the XQuery program reusable, function declarationsProgramming ConstructsFunctions are available. A simple function would be, for example, this one:
UWE test footnote (27/40, callout A): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc. declare function local:wrap-header($json) { xdmp:add-response-header("Pragma", "no-cache"), xdmp:add-response-header("Cache-Control", "no-cache"), xdmp:add-response-header("Expires", "0"), xdmp:set-response-content-type('text/json; charset=utf-8'), $json };
It wraps a JSON string with appropriate header information.
For the function to be included, a matching namespaceXML ConstructsNamespaces must be declared: declare namespace local = 'local:'; | | Rejected by , .
Functions are not only practical for reusability, but also for implementing quite elementary constructs, such as while...do Programming ConstructsLoops - while..do loops.
For this, you use - as is common in functional programming - recursion: declare function local:ist-letzter-wert-in-kette($glied) { let $wert := local:komplizierte-berechnung($glied), $naechstes-glied := local:komplizierte-berechnung-der-position($glied), return if ($naechstes-glied and not($wert = 'foobar')) then local:durchlaufe-kette($naechstes-glied) else $wert = 'foobar' };
In this small snippet, several characteristics of XQuery are already visible. Variable assignments are done with a colon, comparisons on the other hand with just a simple "=". Statements are separated by a comma.

section.title: Function Calls in XPath
section.p: If a function operates on a node set, the function call can also be appended to a path selector, for example like this:
Here, the betrag nodes of a previously selected subtree, stored in the variable $current, are summed up - but only if the value is greater than 0.
The function call here is a type cast to a decimal value to ensure a certain computational precision.
The filtering for positive values is still conventionally formulated:
xs:decimal takes the currently selected node and converts it to a decimal value for comparison with 0.
If an unsupported format is passed to the type constructor xs:decimal, e.g. a string, a fatal error is thrown and the program aborts.
The function call can, however, also be attached as a path selection to an XPath:
In case of an error, the erroneous value is not summed and the program continues.
The conventional way would be to use a loop that maps all values to their decimal value:
This is a bit more complicated, but ensures better robustness of the program.

messagepanel.typeofhazard: Function calls as path selectors do not abort on an error in the function - without explicit exception handling.
messagepanel.howtoavoid: If more robustness is required, you should iterate over result node sets and issue function calls in the conventional way.

section.p: Here, all billings are stored in a collection /abrechnung. The billings with transaction numbers 3,8,9 and 10 are to be retrieved. These billings can also be managed in different collections, e.g. via a collection type "booking". A booking collection gathers all billings that were made on a specific booking date. We now assume that all billings 3,8,9,10 were made on a specific booking date - and only these. For some reason, we want to delete this booking. That is exactly what the above one-liner does. The filter:
returns a node set. The filtered function result
is also a node set. Normally, we would need loops to iterate over these sets. That would look something like this
The source code is much longer this way, but even less experienced XPath experts can easily see what it is about.

section.title: Modules
section.p: To modularizeProgramming ConstructsModules an XQuery application, individual scripts can be outsourced into modules. A module, e.g. common.xqy, is declared via its own namespace:
This module can then be included in other scripts:
Functions and variables are then called with the namespaceXML ConstructsNamespaces prefix:
